text: Grand sustainability challenges are cross-sectoral in nature, often requiring the development of new technologies that will stretch existing policy rules and will lead to the emergence of new policy subsystems. This paper provides a framework to understand these multi-system settings and uses the case of the emerging hydrogen policy subsystem in Germany (where many actors are affiliated to the energy, transport, buildings and industry sectors) to illustrate the new challenges for policy making. It investigates beliefs and coalitions within this new policy subsystem and finds a relatively unusual, yet heterogenous, alternative coalition of companies, civil society organisations and research institutes, versus a more standard coalition consisting of heating and gas companies. Both coalitions disagree about production technologies, applications and the benchmark for the future imports of hydrogen. However, surprisingly, they all agree on the need for hydrogen as an energy carrier and the need for a powerful state to achieve the transition. By linking Advocacy Coalition Framework theory with politics of the sustainability transitions we can explain the emerging coalitions in new policy subsystems, shaped by technology and by sectoral logic, but also by social networks and trust. In so doing so, our paper adds a new level of detail to the understanding of the emergence of new coalitions.
